title: Carved birch skimmer
subtitle: A flat-bowled skimmer with a long handle in green birch
excerpt: A green-birch skimmer with a broad shallow bowl and a long 25 cm handle. Used for lifting froth from jam, foam from stock, or bread dough from a bowl. An intermediate project because the bowl is wide and the walls thin.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for treen construction. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive for domestic skimmer forms. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for blank-shaping method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul rather than sawn. Riven blanks follow the grain perfectly.

content.text: A skimmer is a wide-bowled spoon with a thin wall and a long handle. It wants a flat piece of
green wood

: either a split piece off a larger billet or a wide flat branch section. Birch is ideal. The bowl is wider than an eating spoon, which means the hook knife has more ground to cover, and the walls are thinner (3 mm finished), which requires reading the bowl depth more carefully as you work.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
You need a flat billet 30 cm long and at least 9 cm wide. A
riven
half-section of a birch branch, split down the middle, gives an ideal flat face to start from. The
blank
needs to be at least 18 mm thick to leave room for the convex back of the bowl.
Birch carves cleanly green and the pale colour suits kitchen use. Sycamore is the next option. Both are food-safe after oiling.
Shaping the outline

Draw the skimmer outline on the flat face: a round bowl 8 cm in diameter with a gentle lip, and a handle 22 cm long. The bowl should be at the end of the blank, not in the middle. Use the carving axe to chop away the wood on either side of the handle to reduce it to a rough rectangle. Keep the bowl section intact.

Switch to the sloyd knife. Use push cuts to reduce the handle to an oval cross-section. Work from the bowl neck toward the handle tip: with the grain, clean cuts. The back of the bowl gets a gentle dome of 5 mm from rim to centre; shape this with push cuts working from the widest point toward the rim.
Hollowing the bowl

The large hook knife covers the wide bowl efficiently. Work in arcs from the centre outward. The target depth is 5 to 6 mm from rim to bowl floor. Check by pressing the blade of the sloyd knife flat across the rim and measuring the gap underneath.

The walls of a skimmer are thinner than an eating spoon. 3 mm at the rim. At that thickness, the rim will flex slightly when pressed. Hold the blank up to the light regularly; translucent patches mean the wall is at 2 mm or less. Leave those areas and thin the thick spots to match.
Finishing
Dry in a paper bag for two to three weeks. Sand the bowl interior with 400 grit folded around a finger. Apply raw linseed oil. Wipe the surplus after one hour. Two weeks before using near food.
Variations
Perforated bowl: once dry, bore three or four 5 mm holes through the bowl with a brace and small bit. Allows liquid to drain when skimming.
Sycamore instead of birch: slightly softer, otherwise identical.
Left-handed: the skimmer is symmetrical, so no adjustment needed.
Care
Hand wash and towel-dry. The thin bowl walls are more fragile than a thick-walled spoon. Do not leave the skimmer in washing-up water; the thin rim will check (develop hairline cracks) if it soaks.

rows.cause: Wall too thin in one spot and the hook knife went through
rows.fix: A small hole can be left as a drain hole and the skimmer used as a perforated version. A large hole means starting again. The fix for next time: check the rim thickness every ten minutes with your finger.

rows.symptom: Bowl is not round, one side is flat
rows.cause: Not working the full 360 degrees of the bowl perimeter
rows.fix: Work in a consistent sequence: 12 o'clock, 3 o'clock, 6 o'clock, 9 o'clock, then fill in the quarters. This covers the full circumference evenly.
